documentation_gap_note.issue: The Undercroft package (the first of the five, built before standalone ValidationReport.md files became a standard deliverable) has no dedicated validation report and therefore no formal 'Performance Considerations' section to compile here.
documentation_gap_note.resolution_used: Undercroft's own design document Sec. 14 (Technical World Specification) contains one directly relevant technical note, cited below in performance_strategy. This is a narrower source than the other four districts' full validation-report sections.
documentation_gap_note.recommendation: Consider retrofitting a lightweight Undercroft_ValidationReport.md in a future pass for full consistency across the five packages. Not done here, to avoid modifying the approved Undercroft package's existing file set without explicit instruction.

cross_district_ranking.reasoning: Minimal NPC density and zero combat systems make this the cheapest district in the game to run from a systems-budget perspective.
Low civilian density throughout makes this one of the cheapest locations in the game from an NPC/Mass AI budget perspective.
cross_district_ranking.notes: Tied for cheapest with Sector 0 — both explicitly confirmed cheapest in their own validation reports; this file does not attempt to rank one above the other without a shared source.

cross_district_ranking.reasoning: Low-rise, low-geometric-complexity typology is inherently cheaper to render than the Undercroft's dense hand-placed clutter; no Nanite/Lumen stress concerns.
Best candidate in the game for stress-testing dense hand-placed clutter geometry — irregularity fights modular kit-bashing by default.
The Atrium's gravity-altering, construct-spawning mechanics are the most systemically complex single encounter across all five Phase 1 locations.

priority_rules.note: This ranking is about relative systems/render cost, not narrative importance or build quality. It should not be read as a value judgment on any district.

memory_strategy.note: No numeric memory budget (MB/GB) is specified anywhere in this file. The Builder calculates actual memory allocation at generation time; this file provides only relative prioritization the Builder should weight its allocation by.

forward_looking_engineering_flags.detail: Environmental state changes (shelving, lighting, minor hazards), particularly Tier 3's environmental instability effects, should be reviewed for replication/Blueprint-state-management cost once greyboxed.

The four dynamic lighting states are the one genuine technical novelty relative to the other four districts (none of which have story-tied baseline lighting changes) — primary QA focus should be correctness of these transitions, not performance.
The Atrium's gravity-altering, construct-spawning mechanics should get an early prototype pass independent of art content given their systemic complexity.
The Server Hub's data-scape reality-blur effect needs a technical feasibility check once Sec. 0.1's discrepancy is resolved, particularly if it must support full combat rather than atmosphere alone.
Best candidate in the game for stress-testing dense hand-placed clutter geometry early, since irregularity fights modular kit-bashing by default.
Standing Enforcer NPC density (heavy patrol baseline) should be reviewed for Mass AI crowd-cost budgeting once the flagship instance reaches greybox.
